subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: minerals are naturally occurring inorganic solids with defined composition and measurable properties, identified by conducting tests. Georgia S6E5b: investigate the characteristics of minerals and how minerals contribute to rock composition.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: igneous, metamorphic and sedimentary rocks have unique characteristics used for identification/classification and to interpret formation history. Georgia S6E5c: classify rocks by their formation and explain how rocks change through geologic processes in the rock cycle.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio 6.ESS.2 also ties rock identification to a history of weathering (breakdown) and erosion (transport). Georgia S6E5d: identify types of weathering, agents of erosion and transportation, and environments of deposition.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: igneous, metamorphic and sedimentary rocks form in different ways (cooling magma, heat/pressure, weathering and lithification). Georgia S6E5c: explain how to classify rocks by their formation and how rocks change through the rock cycle.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: soil is unconsolidated material containing organic matter and weathered rock, forming in measurable horizons/layers. Georgia S6E5h: investigate evidence that soil is composed of layers of weathered rocks and decomposed organic material.

subject: science
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: rocks, minerals and soil are geologic resources with practical uses that are nonrenewable. Georgia S6E6a distinguishes renewable/sustainable from nonrenewable resources and how they are used; overlap is the renewable/nonrenewable framing, though GA emphasizes energy resources rather than geologic materials.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: matter is made of atoms; molecules are two or more atoms joined chemically. Georgia S8P1e: analyze the periodic table to illustrate the structure and composition of atoms and simple molecules; GA develops this at greater depth.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: elements are substances of a single kind of atom; molecules combine atoms. Georgia S8P1a: compare and contrast pure substances (elements and compounds) and mixtures; partial overlap on the element/compound concept.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: changes of state explained by a particle model in motion; adding/removing energy causes temperature or phase change. Georgia S8P1b: model the movement of particles in solids, liquids, gases and plasma when thermal energy is added or removed.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: heat is a process of energy transfer and temperature measures average particle motion. Georgia S8P2d: investigate the effects of heat transfer on molecular motion via conduction, radiation and convection; partial overlap on heat-as-transfer and particle motion.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: two categories of energy, kinetic (from motion) and potential (from position). Georgia S8P2a: analyze data relating kinetic energy to mass and speed and potential energy to mass and height; same KE/PE distinction with GA adding quantitative analysis.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: objects have kinetic energy from motion and potential energy from position. Georgia S8P2b: investigate the transformation between kinetic and potential energy within a system; partial overlap extending the KE/PE concept to transformations.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: motion is described by speed and direction, with position and speed graphed as a function of time (no velocity/acceleration). Georgia S8P3a: analyze motion graphs for patterns in speed and distance; GA also introduces velocity and acceleration.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: cells are the fundamental unit of life and all living things are composed of cells. Georgia S5L3a: gather evidence supporting a claim that plants and animals are comprised of cells too small to be seen without magnification.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio 6.LS.2 frames cell reproduction as transmitting genetic information from one generation to the next. Georgia S7L3a: explain the role of genes and chromosomes in inheriting a trait; partial overlap only on the genetic-information portion, not on cell division/growth and repair.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: cells carry out specific functions with specialized parts (membrane, transport, energy, protein building, waste). Georgia S7L2a: explain how cell structures (nucleus, cytoplasm, membrane, wall, chloroplasts, lysosome, mitochondria) work together to obtain nutrients, grow, make materials and process waste.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: cells are covered by a membrane and contain specialized parts. Georgia S5L3b: model and label parts of plant and animal cells (membrane, wall, cytoplasm, nucleus, chloroplasts); partial overlap on identifying cell components.

subject: science
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: levels of organization within organisms include cells, tissues, organs, organ systems and whole organisms. Georgia S7L2b: use a conceptual model of how cells are organized into tissues, tissues into organs, organs into systems, and systems into organisms.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: regions can be classified and compared using landform, climate, population and economic characteristics. Georgia SS6G9a: compare how the location, climate and natural resources of Germany, the UK and Russia impact trade and where people live; GA applies the region-comparison skill to specific countries.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: human activities modify the physical environment in the Eastern Hemisphere. Georgia SS7G2b: explain the relationship between poor soil and deforestation in Sub-Saharan Africa, an instance of humans altering the Eastern-Hemisphere environment.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: the variety of physical environments in the Eastern Hemisphere influences human activities. Georgia SS7G3a: explain how the characteristics of the Sahara, Sahel, savanna and rain forest impact trade and affect where people live.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: major world religions including Christianity, Islam and Judaism impacted the Eastern Hemisphere. Georgia SS7G8c: compare and contrast the prominent religions of Southwest Asia (Judaism, Islam and Christianity).

subject: social_studies
rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: major world religions including Buddhism and Hinduism, plus cultural diffusion, impacted the Eastern Hemisphere. Georgia SS7G12b: compare and contrast the belief systems originating in Southern and Eastern Asia (Buddhism, Hinduism, Shintoism and Confucianism).

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: governments range from monarchies to democracies and citizens' liberties vary with limits on governmental authority. Georgia SS6CG1a: explain citizen participation in autocratic versus democratic governments, reflecting how citizen roles differ by government type.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio categorizes governments including different forms of democracy. Georgia SS6CG1b: describe the two predominant forms of democratic government, parliamentary and presidential; partial overlap on classifying government forms.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: the fundamental questions of economics are what to produce, how to produce and for whom to produce. Georgia SS6E1a: compare how traditional, command and market economies answer what, how and for whom to produce.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: when regions/countries specialize, global trade occurs. Georgia SS6E2a: explain how specialization encourages trade between countries.

subject: social_studies
rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: when selecting items to buy, individuals weigh costs and benefits and compare price and quality. Georgia SS6E13b: understand that a budget is a tool to plan spending and saving of income; partial overlap on personal spending decisions rather than cost/benefit comparison specifically.
